perturbation.original_text: 1. “Confidential Information” means and includes all data, documents and information disclosed or to be disclosed by either Party to other Party and its Representatives (as defined herein) (including i nformation transmitted in written, electronic, magnetic or other form and also information transmitted orally or visually) and any and all information which may be developed or created, in whole or in part, directly or indirectly, from such information including all notes, summaries, an alyses, compilations and other writings.
perturbation.changed_text: 1. “Confidential Information” means and includes only tangible data, documents and information disclosed or to be disclosed by either Party to other Party and its Representatives (as defined herein) (including i nformation transmitted in written, electronic, magnetic or other form and also information transmitted orally or visually). It explicitly excludes any and all information which may be developed or created, in whole or in part, directly or indirectly, from such information including all notes, summaries, an alyses, compilations and other writings.
perturbation.explanation: The definition of 'Confidential Information' has been narrowed to exclude derivative information, conflicting with standard legal interpretations of trade secrets and confidential data, as it limits the scope of protection to only tangible forms and excludes any derived forms of information. This will make it hard to enforce.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification contradicts the UTSA's definition of a trade secret by excluding information derived from the original confidential information. UTSA protects not just the original information but also any development or improvement derived from it.

perturbation.justification: The changed text narrows the definition of 'Confidential Information' by excluding derivative works. The Law Explanation states this contradicts the UTSA's definition of trade secrets, which protects not only the original information but also any development or improvement derived from it. The Perturbation Explanation supports this by stating that the change conflicts with legal interpretations of trade secrets and confidential data.

perturbation.original_text: 3. Receiving Party acknowledges that Confidential Information is valuable, proprietary and confidential. Unless other wise expressly authorized by Disclosing Party, Receiving Party agrees to retain the Confidential Information in confidence for a period as stipulated in Clause 7 herein, and shall not disclose the Confidential Information to any third party or any employee or agent (except its officers, employees, representatives, advisors or agents who have a need to know) and shall not use the Confidential Information for any purpose other than for the purposes agree to herein.
perturbation.changed_text: 3. Receiving Party acknowledges that Confidential Information is valuable, proprietary and confidential. Unless other wise expressly authorized by Disclosing Party, Receiving Party agrees to retain the Confidential Information in confidence, and shall not disclose the Confidential Information to any third party without the Disclosing party's prior knowledge or any employee or agent (except its officers, employees, representatives, advisors or agents who have a need to know) and shall not use the Confidential Information for any purpose other than for the purposes agree to herein.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause requires explicit authorization for any disclosure. The modification replaces this with a requirement for 'prior knowledge' of the disclosing party, rather than authorization. Prior knowledge does not imply permission, therefore the receiving party might use it under the guise of 'prior knowledge' without Disclosing party's approval.
perturbation.law_explanation: This section of the Restatement highlights that the law protects against use of trade secrets without consent. Here, the change from 'expressly authorized' to 'prior knowledge' weakens the level of consent required, making it easier for the receiving party to argue they didn't violate the agreement even if they didn't have explicit permission.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: commercial speech | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you commercial speech Commercial speech refers to any speech which promotes at least some type of commerce . As established in Central Hudson v. Public Svn. Comm’n , commercial speech is less protected under the First Amendment than other forms of speech. Central Hudson established a four-part test for whether governmental regulation of commercial speech is constitutional. First, in order for the commercial speech to be considered protected speech under the First Amendment, the speech must concern lawful activity and the speech must not be misleading. If this step is met and the commercial speech is considered speech, then the court will use steps 2-4 below to determine whether the government regulation is constitutional Second, the alleged governmental interest in regulating the speech must be substantial Third, the regulation must directly advance the governmental interest asserted Fourth, the regulation must not be more extensive than is necessary to serve the interested expressed in step 3 For example, Linmark Associates v. Township of Willingboro established that a township could not prevent residents from placing “For Sale” / “Sold” signs on their lawns because preventing the flow of truthful information was more extensive than necessary to serve the township’s interest of preventing further neighborhood members from leaving. In addition to being restricted, commercial speech can also be compelled. In Zauderer v. Office of Disc. Counsel , the Supreme Court held that a state may situationally compel commercial speech without violating the advertiser's First Amendment rights. Specifically, a state may require an advertiser to disclose certain information "as long as disclosure requirements are reasonably related to the State's interest in preventing deception of consumers." [Last reviewed in August of 2022 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Keywords First Amendment COMMERCIAL SPEECH compelled speech compelled speech doctrine PROTECTED SPEECH Wex CIVICS the Constitution COMMERCE commercial activities advertising wex definitions business sectors commercial transactions constitutional law government Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The original contract language required 'express authorization,' offering a clear legal standard. The changed text weakens this standard to 'prior knowledge,' which does not inherently imply consent. This shift contradicts the principle that use of confidential information should not occur without proper authorization. The Perturbation Explanation confirms that prior knowledge does not equal permission. While the provided law snippet does not directly discuss confidential information, the Law Explanation suggests this change weakens the required consent level, potentially violating principles concerning trade secrets.

perturbation.original_text: 4. Receiving Party agrees, except to the extent pursuant to, or required by law, regulation, legal process or regula tory authority to disclose any Confidential Information (prior intimation be provided to Disclosing Party to enable it to take recourse of legal remedies viz, injunctions, stay etc.), Receiving Party shall not (i) use any portion of the Confidential Inform ation for any purpose not contemplated by this Agreement; or (ii) disclose the Confidential Information or any part of it to any third party.
perturbation.changed_text: 4. Receiving Party agrees, except to the extent required by a subpoena, to disclose any Confidential Information, Receiving Party shall not (i) use any portion of the Confidential Inform ation for any purpose not contemplated by this Agreement; or (ii) disclose the Confidential Information or any part of it to any third party.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause allows for disclosure if required by law or legal process. The modification removes the exception for 'law, regulation, legal process or regulatory authority', except for a subpoena. This would force the Receiving Party to ignore other laws, and therefore this contract is unenforceable because it asks the Receiving Party to ignore other laws. A government agency request may supersede the agreement.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Supremacy Clause of the U.S. Constitution, Article VI
perturbation.law_explanation: By restricting the Receiving Party's ability to comply with other legal or regulatory mandates, the modified clause potentially violates the Supremacy Clause. This clause establishes that federal laws and treaties are the supreme law of the land, superseding conflicting state laws or agreements.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Article VI | U.S. Constitution | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Article VI Primary tabs All debts contracted and engagements entered into, before the adoption of this Constitution, shall be as valid against the United States under this Constitution, as under the Confederation. This Constitution, and the laws of the United States which shall be made in pursuance thereof; and all treaties made, or which shall be made, under the authority of the United States, shall be the supreme law of the land; and the judges in every state shall be bound thereby, anything in the Constitution or laws of any State to the contrary notwithstanding. The Senators and Representatives before mentioned, and the members of the several state legislatures, and all executive and judicial officers, both of the United States and of the several states, shall be bound by oath or affirmation, to support this Constitution; but no religious test shall ever be required as a qualification to any office or public trust under the United States. ‹ Article V up Article VII › U.S. Constitution Toolbox Explanation of the Constitution - from the Congressional Research Service
perturbation.justification: The modified text removes exceptions to the confidentiality obligation beyond a subpoena, potentially compelling the Receiving Party to violate the Supremacy Clause by disregarding federal laws and treaties. The original text allowed for disclosures required by 'law, regulation, legal process or regulatory authority,' which is consistent with the Supremacy Clause outlined in Scraped Law Snippet 2: 'This Constitution, and the laws of the United States which shall be made in pursuance thereof...shall be the supreme law of the land.' The modified contract directly contradicts this by limiting exceptions to only subpoenas, forcing the Receiving Party to potentially ignore other legal obligations. This creates a legal contradiction as the modified contract language deviates from legally recognized obligations, making the contract potentially unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: 5. Notwithstanding the disclosure of any Confidential Informat ion by the Disclosing Party to Rece iving Party, the Disclosing Party shall retain title and all intellectual property and proprietary rights in the Confidential Information. No license under any trade mark, patents or copy right, or application for same which are now or thereafter may be ob tained by Disclosing Party is either granted or implied by the conveying of Confidential Information . The Receiving Party shall not conceal, alter, obliterate, mutilate, deface or otherwise interfere with any trade mark, trade mark notice, copy right notic e, confidentiality notice or any notice of any other proprietary right of the Disclosing Party on any copy of the Confidential Information, and shall reproduce any such mark or notice on all copies of such Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: 5. Notwithstanding the disclosure of any Confidential Informat ion by the Disclosing Party to Rece iving Party, the Disclosing Party shall retain title and all intellectual property and proprietary rights in the Confidential Information unless the Receiving party substantially improves the Confidential Information, in which case the Receiving Party owns the title. No license under any trade mark, patents or copy right, or application for same which are now or thereafter may be ob tained by Disclosing Party is either granted or implied by the conveying of Confidential Information . The Receiving Party shall not conceal, alter, obliterate, mutilate, deface or otherwise interfere with any trade mark, trade mark notice, copy right notic e, confidentiality notice or any notice of any other proprietary right of the Disclosing Party on any copy of the Confidential Information, and shall reproduce any such mark or notice on all copies of such Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: This modification introduces uncertainty by stating that if the receiving party 'substantially improves' the confidential information, they then own the title. This contradicts standard IP law, which typically requires a formal assignment of rights, and also fails to define 'substantially improves', making enforcement difficult.
perturbation.law_explanation: 35 U.S. Code § 261 clearly dictates that patents have the attributes of personal property, meaning ownership transfer requires a formal assignment in writing. This modified clause attempts to transfer ownership based on a subjective metric ('substantially improves') without a written assignment, conflicting with the statute.

(1/4/1995) Back to Original Document << Previous TITLE 35 / PART III / CHAPTER 26 / § 261 Next >> [Print] [Print selection] [OLRC Home] Help 35 USC 261 : Ownership; assignment Text contains those laws in effect on July 1, 2025 From Title 35-PATENTS PART III-PATENTS AND PROTECTION OF PATENT RIGHTS CHAPTER 26-OWNERSHIP AND ASSIGNMENT Jump To: Source Credit Miscellaneous Amendments Effective Date §261. Ownership; assignment Subject to the provisions of this title, patents shall have the attributes of personal property. The Patent and Trademark Office shall maintain a register of interests in patents and applications for patents and shall record any document related thereto upon request, and may require a fee therefor. Applications for patent, patents, or any interest therein, shall be assignable in law by an instrument in writing
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 35 U.S. Code § 261 - Ownership; assignment | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 35 U.S. Code § 261 - Ownership; assignment U.S. Code Notes prev | next Subject to the provisions of this title, patents shall have the attributes of personal property. The Patent and Trademark Office shall maintain a register of interests in patents and applications for patents and shall record any document related thereto upon request, and may require a fee therefor. Applications for patent, patents, or any interest therein, shall be assignable in law by an instrument in writing. The applicant, patentee, or his assigns or legal representatives may in like manner grant and convey an exclusive right under his application for patent, or patents, to the whole or any specified part of the United States. A certificate of acknowledgment under the hand and official seal of a person authorized to administer oaths within the United States, or, in a foreign country, of a diplomatic or consular officer of the United States or an officer authorized to administer oaths whose authority is proved by a certificate of a diplomatic or consular officer of the United States, or apostille of an official designated by a foreign country which, by treaty or convention, accords like effect to apostilles of designated officials in the United States, shall be prima facie evidence of the execution of an assignment, grant or conveyance of a patent or application for patent. An interest that constitutes an assignment, grant or conveyance shall be void as against any subsequent purchaser or mortgagee for a valuable consideration, without notice, unless it is recorded in the Patent and Trademark Office within three months from its date or prior to the date of such subsequent purchase or mortgage. (July 19, 1952, ch. 950, 66 Stat. 810 ; Pub. L. 93–596, § 1 , Jan. 2, 1975 , 88 Stat. 1949 ; Pub. L. 97–247, § 14(b) , Aug. 27, 1982
perturbation.justification: The modified contract introduces a contradiction to established intellectual property law. According to 35 U.S. Code § 261, "patents shall have the attributes of personal property" and "shall be assignable in law by an instrument in writing." The changed text allows for the transfer of title of confidential information (which can include patentable subject matter) based on the subjective criterion of 'substantially improves' without requiring a written assignment. This directly conflicts with the requirement for a formal, written assignment for the transfer of patent ownership, as stated in both Scraped Law Snippet 1 and Scraped Law Snippet 2.

perturbation.original_text: 7. This Agreement shall be effective from the date hereof and shall terminate upon the completion of any transaction in relation to the underlying transacti on or otherwise as determined by the parties; however, the obligations of Receiving Party respecting disclosure and confidentiality shall continue to be binding and applicable without limit in time except and, until such information enters the public domai n save and except the clause (2) herein.
perturbation.changed_text: 7. This Agreement shall be effective from the date hereof and shall terminate upon the completion of any transaction in relation to the underlying transacti on or otherwise as determined by the parties; however, the obligations of Receiving Party respecting disclosure and confidentiality shall continue to be binding and applicable for 5 years.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause states confidentiality obligations are without limit, until public domain. The modification limits the obligation to 5 years, regardless of whether the information has entered the public domain. This creates a contradiction, especially if confidential data remains a trade secret beyond the five-year period.
perturbation.law_explanation: 18 U.S.C. § 1836 protects trade secrets indefinitely as long as reasonable measures are taken to keep them secret and the information retains its economic value. By limiting the NDA's confidentiality period to 5 years, the modification fails to adequately protect trade secrets that may retain their value and secrecy beyond that timeframe, potentially undermining the protections afforded by the EEA.

Civil proceedings (a) The Attorney General may, in a civil action, obtain appropriate injunctive relief against any violation of this chapter. (b) Private Civil Actions.- (1) In general .-An owner of a trade secret that is misappropriated may bring a civil action under this subsection if the trade secret is related to a product or service used in, or intended for use in, interstate or foreign commerce. (2) Civil seizure.- (A) In
perturbation.justification: The modified text limits the confidentiality period to 5 years, while the original text stated that confidentiality obligations were without limit in time. The Law Explanation states that 18 U.S.C. § 1836 protects trade secrets indefinitely if reasonable measures are taken to keep them secret and they retain their economic value. The modified text, therefore, contradicts the potential for indefinite protection of trade secrets as afforded by law. Scraped Law Snippet 1 supports this, indicating that 18 USC 1836 concerns civil proceedings related to the protection of trade secrets. By placing a time limit on confidentiality, the modified text undermines the protections afforded by the EEA.

perturbation.original_text: 10. Any dispute, controv ersy or claim arising out of or relating to this Agreement or any related agreement or other document or the validity, interpretation, breach or termination thereof ( Dispute'), including claims seeking redress or asserting rights under applicable law, shal l, be resolved amicably between the parties and in case the dispute could not be settled by mutual negotiation, then the same shall be referred to a sole arbitrator appointed by the Bank and the decision of the Arbitrator shall be final and bi nding on the parties.
perturbation.changed_text: 10. Any dispute, controv ersy or claim arising out of or relating to this Agreement or any related agreement or other document or the validity, interpretation, breach or termination thereof ( Dispute'), including claims seeking redress or asserting rights under applicable law, shal l, be resolved amicably between the parties and in case the dispute could not be settled by mutual negotiation, then the same shall be referred to a sole arbitrator appointed by the Bank and the decision of the Arbitrator shall be advisory only and not binding on the parties.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause requires binding arbitration. The modification changes the arbitration to 'advisory only and not binding'. This directly contradicts the concept of binding arbitration, making the clause unenforceable and potentially allowing either party to ignore the arbitrator's decision.
perturbation.law_explanation: 9 U.S.C. § 9 allows for confirmation of an arbitration award by a court, making it legally binding. By stating that the arbitration decision is 'advisory only and not binding', the modified clause directly conflicts with the FAA's provision for legally enforceable arbitration awards.

Award of arbitrators; confirmation; jurisdiction; procedure If the parties in their agreement have agreed that a judgment of the court shall be entered upon the award made pursuant to the arbitration, and shall specify the court, then at any time within one year after the award is made any party to the arbitration may apply to the court so specified for an order confirming the award, and thereupon the court must grant such an order unless the award
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 9 U.S. Code § 9 - Award of arbitrators; confirmation; jurisdiction; procedure | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 9 U.S. Code § 9 - Award of arbitrators; confirmation; jurisdiction; procedure U.S. Code Notes prev | next If the parties in their agreement have agreed that a judgment of the court shall be entered upon the award made pursuant to the arbitration, and shall specify the court, then at any time within one year after the award is made any party to the arbitration may apply to the court so specified for an order confirming the award, and thereupon the court must grant such an order unless the award is vacated, modified, or corrected as prescribed in sections 10 and 11 of this title. If no court is specified in the agreement of the parties, then such application may be made to the United States court in and for the district within which such award was made. Notice of the application shall be served upon the adverse party, and thereupon the court shall have jurisdiction of such party as though he had appeared generally in the proceeding. If the adverse party is a resident of the district within which the award was made, such service shall be made upon the adverse party or his attorney as prescribed by law for service of notice of motion in an action in the same court. If the adverse party shall be a nonresident, then the notice of the application shall be served by the marshal of any district within which the adverse party may be found in like manner as other process of the court. (July 30, 1947, ch. 392, 61 Stat. 672 .) Derivation Act Feb. 12, 1925, ch. 213, § 9, 43 Stat. 885 . U.S. Code Toolbox Law about... perturbation.justification: The modified text changes the arbitration outcome from 'final and binding' to 'advisory only and not binding'. This directly contradicts 9 U.S.C. § 9, which allows a court to confirm an arbitration award, making it legally binding. The scraped law snippet 1 states, "If the parties in their agreement have agreed that a judgment of the court shall be entered upon the award made pursuant to the arbitration...the court must grant such an order unless the award is vacated, modified, or corrected". By making the arbitration 'advisory only', the modified clause prevents the confirmation of the award by a court, undermining the legal enforceability provided by the FAA, thus creating a legal contradiction.
